the number $ \frac{100!}{10^{50}} $ can be written as an irreducible fraction. to do this, we need to understand the prime factorization of both the numerator and the denominator. the numerator is 100 factorial, which includes all integers from 1 to 100. the denominator is $ 10^{50} $, which can be expressed as $ 2^{50} \times 5^{50} $. this helps in simplifying the fraction by canceling out common factors.

***

to find the irreducible form of the fraction, we need to count how many times the prime numbers 2 and 5 appear in the prime factorization of 100!. this is done using legendre’s formula. for each prime $ p $, the exponent in $ n! $ is given by $ \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \left\lfloor \frac{n}{p^k} \right\rfloor $. applying this to 2 and 5 in 100! gives us the total number of 2s and 5s in the numerator.

***

after calculating, we find that the number of 2s in 100! is much higher than the number of 5s. this means that when we divide by $ 10^{50} $, which removes 50 2s and 50 5s, the remaining number of 2s will still be greater than the remaining number of 5s. this is important because the denominator of the irreducible fraction will be determined by the remaining 5s after the division.

***

the key idea here is that the denominator of the irreducible fraction is the product of all primes that are not canceled out by the numerator. since 100! contains more 2s than 5s, after removing 50 of each, the number of 5s will be less than the number of 2s. this means that the denominator will be composed of the remaining 5s and any other primes that are not canceled out by the numerator.

***
